payload.title: SWEEP-AP: consumeNonce prunes the entire nonce map on every request under the global transport lock
payload.context:
Location: pkg/transport/http/transport.go, consumeNonce() lines 373-393

Anti-pattern: O(n) full-map scan under a global exclusive lock on every request

consumeNonce() holds t.mu.Lock() (the transport-global write lock, shared with AddPeer, RemovePeer, session management, and SelfInfo) and iterates over the entire seenNonces map to prune expired entries on every single request. The nonce window is 2×60s, so in a 120-request-per-second scenario the map holds up to ~14,400 entries, all scanned on every request.

The t.mu lock is the same lock used by the sign and rekey handlers — contention from nonce pruning blocks concurrent FROST signing operations.

Why it matters: The scan was likely fine for low-traffic scenarios, but the interaction with the global lock means nonce pruning adds latency to all concurrent signed operations, not just the current request. This was flagged in the SSRF cleanup but the lock scope was not addressed.

Refactor direction: Use a separate sync.Mutex for nonces (decoupling from t.mu), and prune lazily using a cheaper strategy: maintain a ring buffer of (nonce, expiresAt) pairs or use a periodic background goroutine. At minimum, decouple the nonce lock from t.mu so FROST sessions and nonce checks do not serialize.

Severity: medium — latency impact under concurrent load; no correctness issue.

Close reason: Implemented: decoupled nonce lock from global transport mutex. Added nonceMu (sync.Mutex) to Transport, background pruner goroutine in Start/Stop, and lazy-prune fast path. consumeNonce now holds nonceMu instead of t.mu, eliminating serialization with FROST signing operations.

import_history.note: Implemented: decoupled nonce lock from global transport mutex. Added nonceMu (sync.Mutex) to Transport, background pruner goroutine in Start/Stop, and lazy-prune fast path. consumeNonce now holds nonceMu instead of t.mu, eliminating serialization with FROST signing operations.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: role vocabulary split — campfire package defines RoleObserver/Writer/Full but store/cmd use "creator"/"member" without constants
payload.context:
Location: pkg/campfire/campfire.go (RoleObserver, RoleWriter, RoleFull constants), vs cmd/cf/cmd/create.go, evict.go, disband.go, dm.go, swarm_start.go (bare "creator"), and pkg/store/store.go PeerEndpoint.Role comment ("creator", "member", "writer", "observer")

Anti-pattern: Incomplete constant coverage — two parallel role vocabularies with no shared source of truth

The campfire package defines three role constants (RoleObserver, RoleWriter, RoleFull) but the codebase also uses two additional role strings ("creator" and "member") as bare string literals across multiple files. The EffectiveRole() function in campfire.go maps "creator" and "member" → RoleFull, but:
1. "creator" is used as a PeerEndpoint.Role value in store.PeerEndpoint — a different role namespace from the campfire membership role.
2. "member" is used as a default fallback in store.GetPeerRole() and store.ListPeerEndpoints() — again a peer-role, not a membership role.
3. The role enforcement in handleDeliver (handler_message.go) only handles "observer" and "writer" — "creator" and "member" both fall through as unrestricted, which is correct but not explicit.

The two namespaces (membership role: "full"/"writer"/"observer"; peer endpoint role: "creator"/"member") are semantically distinct but use the same Role field on different types, with no type or constant separation.

Why it matters: A developer adding a new role or checking role-based access must understand both vocabularies, which are not documented together anywhere. The EffectiveRole function silently absorbs "creator" → "full" which works but hides the intended semantics.

Refactor direction: Either (a) define PeerRole constants alongside the campfire constants ("creator", "member") and use them in store operations; or (b) use separate types (type MembershipRole string, type PeerRole string) to make the two namespaces explicit. At minimum, document the two-namespace model in store.go or campfire.go.

Severity: medium — maintenance confusion and implicit type-unsafety, but no current correctness bug.

Close reason: Implemented: defined PeerRoleCreator/PeerRoleMember constants in pkg/store, replaced all bare 'creator' and 'member' string literals in store.go and cmd/ files with constants, added two-namespace documentation to both campfire.go and store.go. Tests pass.

import_history.note: Implemented: defined PeerRoleCreator/PeerRoleMember constants in pkg/store, replaced all bare 'creator' and 'member' string literals in store.go and cmd/ files with constants, added two-namespace documentation to both campfire.go and store.go. Tests pass.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: compact --before uses timestamp==0 sentinel for message-not-found

import_history.note: Implemented: replaced beforeTS==0 sentinel with matchedID=="" in execCompact. Added 2 regression tests. Commit ccdbd97.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: Poll() uses a plain http.Client without SSRF-safe transport
payload.context:
## Bug

The Poll() function in peer.go (line 422) creates a per-request plain http.Client:

```go
pollClient := &http.Client{Timeout: time.Duration(timeoutSecs+5) * time.Second}
resp, err := pollClient.Do(req)
```

The package-level httpClient (line 30-33) correctly uses newSSRFSafeTransport() to protect against DNS rebinding SSRF attacks. But Poll() intentionally bypasses this by creating a new plain http.Client (because the 30s global timeout is too short for long-polls).

## Scenario

A malicious peer (or compromised DNS) can return an endpoint that resolves to a private IP at connection time, bypassing the validation-time IP check in validateJoinerEndpoint. The Poll() path specifically does NOT use the SSRF-safe transport, leaving the time-of-use DNS rebinding protection absent for long-poll connections.

The initial endpoint validation by validateJoinerEndpoint runs at join time (validation of JoinerEndpoint in handleJoin). But the actual Poll() connection happens later — any DNS rebinding in the window between validation and connection bypasses the protection.

## Fix approach

Create an SSRF-safe transport with a longer timeout instead of using the default transport:
```go
pollTransport := newSSRFSafeTransport() // same transport, SSRF-safe dialer
pollClient := &http.Client{
    Timeout:   time.Duration(timeoutSecs+5) * time.Second,
    Transport: pollTransport,
}
```

## Severity

Medium — DNS rebinding requires attacker control of DNS and a specific timing window. The endpoint was already validated at join time. However, the SSRF-safe transport exists precisely to close this window and Poll() is the one path that bypasses it.

## Location

/pkg/transport/http/peer.go Poll() function, line 422

Close reason: Implemented: Poll() now uses pollTransport (newSSRFSafeTransport() by default) instead of a plain http.Client, closing the DNS-rebinding TOCTOU window. Added OverridePollTransportForTest() for test compatibility. Committed 670fe01 on work/workspace-pm9m.5.3.

import_history.note: Implemented: Poll() now uses pollTransport (newSSRFSafeTransport() by default) instead of a plain http.Client, closing the DNS-rebinding TOCTOU window. Added OverridePollTransportForTest() for test compatibility. Committed 670fe01 on work/workspace-pm9m.5.3.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: handleDeliver role enforcement uses msg.Tags ([]string) not typed check — writer tag bypass
payload.context:
## Bug

handleDeliver in handler_message.go (line 67-73) enforces the writer role restriction by checking msg.Tags directly using strings.HasPrefix:

```go
case "writer":
    for _, tag := range msg.Tags {
        if strings.HasPrefix(tag, "campfire:") {
            // reject
        }
    }
```

This is server-side enforcement that correctly blocks writers from delivering campfire:* system messages. However, the check iterates msg.Tags which are the Tags from the decoded CBOR message — before any normalization. 

The message was verified by msg.VerifySignature() before this check. The signature verification uses MessageSignInput which includes msg.Tags. So the tags in msg.Tags are signature-verified.

The actual issue: the role enforcement in handler_message.go at line 67 uses the live message's Tags. But the role is looked up via GetPeerRole which returns "member" as default (line 901 in store.go). If a peer's role is not stored (e.g., they joined before role enforcement was added), GetPeerRole returns "member", not "writer" or "observer". This means the default fallback to "member" bypasses the writer restriction for any peer whose role was not explicitly stored.

## Scenario

1. Agent joins a campfire before role enforcement was implemented (CreatorPubkey empty, role not stored in peer_endpoints).
2. GetPeerRole returns "member" (the backward-compatible default).
3. The switch statement falls through to neither "observer" nor "writer" cases — no restriction applied.
4. The agent can deliver any message including campfire:* system messages.

This is a backward-compatibility regression from the role enforcement sweep: the "full"/"creator" role values from store.AddMembership are stored in campfire_memberships.role, but peer_endpoints.role defaults to "member" (not "creator" or "full"). So a peer who is actually the creator may have role="member" in peer_endpoints, and pass all restrictions.

## Severity

Medium — in practice, server-side enforcement is secondary to auth (must be a member). The creator always has client-side full access. But the server role enforcement is not reliable for the "observer"/"writer" distinction when roles are missing from peer_endpoints.

## Location

/pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go handleDeliver(), lines 55-75
/pkg/store/store.go GetPeerRole(), line 891 (returns "member" as default, not "full")

Close reason: Implemented: handleDeliver now uses campfire.EffectiveRole() to normalize raw role values before switching. This ensures 'member', 'creator', and '' all map to RoleFull (no restrictions) via explicit semantics rather than implicit switch fallthrough. Added TestDeliverLegacyMemberRoleSystemMessageAllowed to verify legacy 'member' role can deliver system messages. Commit e85bf79.

import_history.note: Implemented: handleDeliver now uses campfire.EffectiveRole() to normalize raw role values before switching. This ensures 'member', 'creator', and '' all map to RoleFull (no restrictions) via explicit semantics rather than implicit switch fallthrough. Added TestDeliverLegacyMemberRoleSystemMessageAllowed to verify legacy 'member' role can deliver system messages. Commit e85bf79.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: compact --before includes the 'before' message itself in superseded set
payload.context:
## Bug

In execCompact (compact.go line 139-143), the filtering uses `msg.Timestamp >= beforeTS` to exclude the before message and everything at or after it:

```go
for _, msg := range allMsgs {
    if msg.Timestamp >= beforeTS || isCompactionMsg(msg) {
        continue
    }
    toSupersede = append(toSupersede, msg)
}
```

The condition `msg.Timestamp >= beforeTS` excludes messages with the same timestamp as the before-message. This is correct for the before-message itself. BUT: if two messages have the exact same nanosecond timestamp (a realistic scenario on fast machines or in tests), both will be excluded from the superseded set — even if one should be included.

More critically: `msg.Timestamp >= beforeTS` uses `>=`, so the before-message itself (matched by `msg.ID == beforeMsgID`) is excluded. The semantic of `--before <msg-id>` is "compact everything BEFORE this message". The before-message should NOT be superseded. This behavior appears intentional.

However: if the before-message has the same timestamp as other messages that SHOULD be superseded (messages chronologically before it that happen to have the same nanosecond timestamp due to concurrent writes), those messages will be incorrectly excluded from the compaction.

## Scenario

1. Two messages M1 and M2 are created with the same nanosecond timestamp (clock resolution on the host, or in fast tests).
2. `cf compact --before M2` intends to supersede M1 and earlier messages.
3. Since M1.Timestamp == M2.Timestamp == beforeTS, the condition `M1.Timestamp >= beforeTS` is true for M1.
4. M1 is skipped — it is NOT added to toSupersede.
5. The compaction event references no messages, triggering "no messages to compact" error OR silently produces incomplete compaction.

## Severity

Medium — nanosecond timestamp collisions are uncommon in production but common in tests. This was likely present before the sweep but may have been made more prominent by the timestamp-based filtering refactoring.

## Location

/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go line 140, execCompact function

Close reason: Implemented: changed timestamp >= beforeTS to (msg.ID == matchedID || msg.Timestamp > beforeTS) to handle nanosecond timestamp collisions. Added TestCompactBeforeTimestampCollision regression test. Commit 974d488.

import_history.note: Implemented: changed timestamp >= beforeTS to (msg.ID == matchedID || msg.Timestamp > beforeTS) to handle nanosecond timestamp collisions. Added TestCompactBeforeTimestampCollision regression test. Commit 974d488.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: handleMembership evict + handleRekey creator check bypassed when CreatorPubkey is empty (legacy campfire privilege escalation)
payload.context:
## Finding

Location:
- pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go line 296 (handleMembership evict case)
- pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go line 93 (handleRekey creator check)

Severity: medium

## Vulnerability

Both handleMembership (evict) and handleRekey use the same pattern for creator-only enforcement:

    if membership != nil && membership.CreatorPubkey != "" && senderHex != membership.CreatorPubkey {
        // reject
    }

This condition is fail-OPEN: if membership.CreatorPubkey == "" (legacy record, stored before creator_pubkey column was added), the creator check is skipped entirely and the eviction/rekey proceeds for ANY authenticated campfire member.

The comment in handleMembership says 'Fail-closed: if we can't verify the creator, reject the eviction' — but the code does the opposite.

## Attack Scenario

1. A legacy campfire was created when the creator_pubkey column did not exist. The creator_pubkey field is empty in campfire_memberships.
2. Any authenticated member (not just the creator) sends POST /campfire/{id}/membership with body {"event":"evict","member":"<victim-pubkey>"}.
3. The server passes the membership check (they're a member), and skips the creator check because CreatorPubkey == "".
4. The victim is removed from the server's in-memory peer list (h.transport.RemovePeer).
5. Same attack works against handleRekey: any member can trigger a rekey, causing the server to accept new key material and rename the campfire.

## Impact

- Unauthorized eviction from peer list (service disruption)
- Unauthorized campfire rekey (key material replacement, campfire rename) on legacy campfires

## Fix Direction

Change the condition to fail-closed: if CreatorPubkey is empty (cannot verify), reject the privileged operation.

    if membership == nil || membership.CreatorPubkey == "" || senderHex != membership.CreatorPubkey {
        http.Error(w, "only the campfire creator may evict members", http.StatusForbidden)
        return
    }

Apply the same pattern to handleRekey. Add a test with a legacy membership record (empty CreatorPubkey) verifying that eviction and rekey are rejected.

Close reason: Implemented: fail-closed creator check for evict and rekey — changed condition to reject when CreatorPubkey is empty, updated test in evict_auth_test.go and added TestRekeyRejectedWhenCreatorPubkeyEmpty. Commit df4a955.

import_history.note: Implemented: fail-closed creator check for evict and rekey — changed condition to reject when CreatorPubkey is empty, updated test in evict_auth_test.go and added TestRekeyRejectedWhenCreatorPubkeyEmpty. Commit df4a955.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: swarm status member count double-counts self for p2p-http campfires
payload.context:
## Bug

In swarm_status.go line 57:
```go
fmt.Printf("Members:        %d\n", len(peers)+1)
```

The +1 is intended to add self (the local agent) since ListPeerEndpoints returns OTHER peers. However, for p2p-http campfires, createP2PHTTP (create.go line 262) stores the creator's own endpoint in peer_endpoints. Similarly, joinP2PHTTP (join.go line 241) stores self in peer_endpoints.

So for p2p-http campfires, `len(peers)` already includes self, and the +1 double-counts the creator.

## Scenario

1. Agent creates p2p-http campfire. Self is stored in peer_endpoints.
2. One other agent joins. That agent is also stored in peer_endpoints.
3. len(peers) = 2. swarm status shows "Members: 3" — off by 1.

For filesystem campfires, self is NOT stored in peer_endpoints, so len(peers)+1 is correct.

## Severity

Low — display bug only. No functional impact.

## Location

/cmd/cf/cmd/swarm_status.go line 57

Close reason: Implemented: fixed swarm status member count double-counting for p2p-http campfires. Used requireAgentAndStore() to get local pubkey, check if self is in ListPeerEndpoints, only add +1 when self not already counted. Commit 80248cc.

import_history.note: Implemented: fixed swarm status member count double-counting for p2p-http campfires. Used requireAgentAndStore() to get local pubkey, check if self is in ListPeerEndpoints, only add +1 when self not already counted. Commit 80248cc.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: collectSupersededIDs has TOCTOU between maxCompactionTimestamp and write lock
payload.context:
## Bug

In collectSupersededIDs (store.go line 646-696), the pattern for the cache-miss rebuild path is:

```go
maxTS, err := s.maxCompactionTimestamp(campfireID)  // (1) read DB, no lock held
...
s.supersededMu.RLock()
entry, ok := s.supersededCache[campfireID]
s.supersededMu.RUnlock()
if ok && entry.maxCompactionTS == maxTS {
    // cache hit: return copy
}

// cache miss: rebuild
events, err := s.ListCompactionEvents(campfireID)    // (2) read DB, no lock held
...
s.supersededMu.Lock()
s.supersededCache[campfireID] = supersededCacheEntry{  // (3) write lock: store
    maxCompactionTS: maxTS,
    superseded:      superseded,
}
s.supersededMu.Unlock()
```

The TOCTOU window: between step (1) (querying maxTS) and step (3) (storing the cache entry under that maxTS), a new compaction event can be:
- Inserted via AddMessage → which deletes cache[campfireID] under write lock

If the insert happens between steps (1) and (3):
1. maxTS is read as T1 (old value)
2. New compaction event inserted → cache[campfireID] deleted
3. Cache entry rebuilt with maxCompactionTS=T1, but the actual max is now T2 > T1
4. The cache entry for campfireID is stored with a STALE maxTS

## Impact

The next call to collectSupersededIDs will see maxTS=T2 from the DB (step 1), which does NOT match entry.maxCompactionTS=T1, causing a cache miss and rebuild. So the stale entry is immediately evicted on the next call — the stale entry can only be returned if:
- maxTS is re-queried at step (1) and returns T1 (before the new event was committed)
- The cache entry with T1 is stored at step (3) AFTER the AddMessage delete

Given SQLite's serialization and the code structure, this window is very narrow. But it is technically a correctness issue: for a brief period after a compaction event is inserted, collectSupersededIDs may return a result that doesn't include the newest compaction event. Any ListMessages call in that window would not exclude messages superseded by the newest event.

## Note

The workspace-zqdc comment says this was fixed by moving invalidation to AddMessage. The fix is correct for the "stale cache hit" scenario it addresses. But the TOCTOU between maxCompactionTimestamp and the write lock to store the rebuilt entry is a new window introduced by that fix's structure. The previous fix assumed that "any compaction event inserted after the cache check will have already invalidated the entry" — but the invalidation happens BEFORE the cache entry is stored (step 3), not before step (1).

## Severity

Medium — the window is narrow and self-correcting (next call will miss and rebuild). Messages are never permanently lost — they remain in the DB. The only observable effect is that ListMessages(RespectCompaction=true) may briefly return superseded messages that should be filtered.

## Location

/pkg/store/store.go collectSupersededIDs function, lines 646-696

Close reason: Implemented: re-query maxCompactionTimestamp inside the write lock before storing the rebuilt cache entry; if timestamp advanced (concurrent compaction insert), skip caching to avoid stale entry. Tests green.

import_history.note: Implemented: re-query maxCompactionTimestamp inside the write lock before storing the rebuilt cache entry; if timestamp advanced (concurrent compaction insert), skip caching to avoid stale entry. Tests green.

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: TestRekeyPhase1ReplayOverwritesSession is unconditionally skipped — session overwrite attack path untested
payload.context:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/rekey_replay_test.go line 101

TestRekeyPhase1ReplayOverwritesSession has t.Skip("TODO: fix ECDH key agreement mismatch between test and handler — test was generated against pre-refactor code") at line 101. This means the phase-1 replay attack path has zero mechanical test coverage.

The attack: an attacker who can observe or predict a sender's X25519 pub key can race a second phase-1 request with the same senderX25519Pub, overwriting the receiver's cached ephemeral key. If the legitimate sender then sends a phase-2 using the shared secret derived from the OLD receiver key, the decryption will fail. The test was written to verify this behavior but was never fixed after the ECDH refactor.

The session management code was changed (storeRekeySession/claimRekeySession gained a composite key including senderEdPubKey to prevent cross-sender overwrites), but the test still uses the pre-refactor API.

Gap type: Skipped security test — critical attack path
Severity: High (security regression risk: if session management regresses, replay-overwrite is silently untested)

What test would close it: Fix the test to use the updated API (cfhttp.SendRekeyPhase1 with proper identity signing). Remove the t.Skip. Verify the overwrite behavior matches the current implementation.

Close reason: Implemented: removed t.Skip from TestRekeyPhase1ReplayOverwritesSession and applied testHKDFSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') to both stale and fresh shared secrets before AES-GCM encryption, matching server-side handler key derivation. Both rekey replay tests pass.

import_history.note: Implemented: removed t.Skip from TestRekeyPhase1ReplayOverwritesSession and applied testHKDFSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') to both stale and fresh shared secrets before AES-GCM encryption, matching server-side handler key derivation. Both rekey replay tests pass.

payload.title: Security/Bug: isCompactionEvent uses substring match — false positive on tag like 'not-campfire:compact'
payload.context:
SECURITY + BUG FINDING from workspace-o3l.6

Both isCompactionEvent (store.go:375) and isCompactionMsg (compact.go:239) use:
  strings.Contains(m.Tags, `"campfire:compact"`)

This is a JSON string contains check. A tag like "not-campfire:compact" or "xycampfire:compact" would match because the JSON encoding of the tags array would contain the substring "campfire:compact".

While the attack surface is limited (only affects compaction filtering, not auth), a message with tag "bad-campfire:compact-extension" would be incorrectly treated as a compaction event and always shown even when RespectCompaction=true.

Files:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go:375 (isCompactionEvent)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:239 (isCompactionMsg)

Fix: Use proper JSON array parsing to check for exact tag match, not substring. OR use json_each SQL approach (already done in ListCompactionEvents query — copy that pattern).

Done condition: Both functions use exact array element comparison. Tests verify a tag "xycampfire:compact" does not cause a message to be treated as a compaction event.

Close reason: Fixed: HasTag with JSON parse replaces strings.Contains. Commit e64a658.

payload.title: SEC-REVIEW: validateMessageToSign allows structurally-valid but semantically-forged HopSignInput — signing oracle partially open
payload.context:
Reviewer: workspace-pm9m.1
Parent: workspace-pm9m

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler_sign.go:24-46

Severity: minor (design limitation acknowledged in bead spec)

Description:
validateMessageToSign() accepts any CBOR bytes that decode to a HopSignInput with non-empty MessageID and CampfireID, or a MessageSignInput with non-empty ID. It does NOT verify:
1. That the MessageID exists in the store (could be a fabricated ID)
2. That the CampfireID is a real campfire this node belongs to
3. That the Payload, Tags, Antecedents fields match any stored message

A campfire member can request this node to co-sign a HopSignInput with:
- MessageID: "<any-uuid>"
- CampfireID: <target-campfire-pubkey-bytes>

This produces a valid threshold signature over a provenance hop for a campfire this node may not even belong to, as long as this node holds a FROST share for the signing campfire. The bead spec (workspace-epwh) described this as the mitigation ("validate that MessageToSign has an expected structure") and the implementation satisfies the spec. However, the remaining attack surface should be noted: structural validation alone does not prevent provenance fraud for fabricated MessageIDs.

This is a design limitation of the fix scope, not an implementation bug. The bead spec explicitly said "the only mitigation would be to validate that MessageToSign has an expected structure."

Done condition (for a deeper fix): Cross-reference MessageID against the local message store; reject HopSignInput for MessageIDs that do not exist locally. This would close the fabrication attack at the cost of requiring the node to have received the message before co-signing its provenance hop.

Close reason: Implemented: cross-reference HopSignInput.MessageID against local message store in validateMessageToSign; rejects fabricated IDs not in store. Updated TestHandleSignValidHopSignInputAccepted to insert message first. Added TestHandleSignFabricatedHopMessageIDRejected negative test. All sign handler tests pass.

import_history.note: Implemented: cross-reference HopSignInput.MessageID against local message store in validateMessageToSign; rejects fabricated IDs not in store. Updated TestHandleSignValidHopSignInputAccepted to insert message first. Added TestHandleSignFabricatedHopMessageIDRejected negative test. All sign handler tests pass.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: handleJoin persists joiner endpoint before membership check completes for invite-only campfires
payload.context:
In handler_join.go handleJoin(), the joiner's endpoint is persisted via h.store.UpsertPeerEndpoint() and h.transport.AddPeer() unconditionally at line 264-270, after the join response is fully built. The invite-only check at line 128-147 correctly rejects unauthorized joiners — but only after the signature is verified. If the campfire is open protocol (no invite-only check), an unauthenticated joiner's pubkey+endpoint is stored in peer_endpoints immediately after signature verification passes, before any other membership validation.

The deeper problem: for invite-only campfires, the membership check at line 130 checks ListPeerEndpoints — but this is the same table that is about to be written. A concurrent join attempt from the same pubkey could race: one request reads the peer list (not yet present, so not admitted), while a concurrent request writes the peer endpoint. Both would be rejected, but the written record would remain in peer_endpoints even for a rejected join.

Trigger: concurrent join attempts from the same pubkey to an invite-only campfire.
Impact: P2 - rejected joiners can leave stale peer endpoint records in the store, polluting the peer list.

Close reason: Fixed: handleJoin now calls DeletePeerEndpoint in the invite-only rejection path to clean up stale peer endpoint records. Added 4 regression tests. All tests green. Commit: 055ea16 on branch worktree-agent-a0721651.

import_history.note: Fixed: handleJoin now calls DeletePeerEndpoint in the invite-only rejection path to clean up stale peer endpoint records. Added 4 regression tests. All tests green. Commit: 055ea16 on branch worktree-agent-a0721651.

payload.title: TEST-GAP: no negative test for sign handler membership check — non-member can trigger FROST round 1
payload.context:
The FROST sign handler (handleSign in handler.go) performs auth header verification (Ed25519 signature check) but there is NO membership check — any authenticated peer can initiate a signing session for any campfire, even if they are not a member.

The e2e test TestFROSTSign2of3OverHTTP only tests the happy path where all three participants are legitimate members. There is no test that:
1. A non-member agent (no peer_endpoint record) sends a POST /campfire/{id}/sign → expects 403.
2. An authenticated agent for a DIFFERENT campfire cannot create sessions in this campfire.

This is a gap in the security test matrix. The poll handler has a membership check (handlePoll lines 267-283); sign does not. Whether this is intentional (signing is always between known members who share DKG state) or a missing check is unclear from the tests alone.

**Done condition:** Either add a test confirming non-members are rejected from /sign, or create a note in the spec that /sign intentionally omits membership checks with justification (the FROST share lookup fails for non-participants anyway).

**Source:** workspace-17qu.13 review finding.

Close reason: Added two negative membership tests for the FROST sign handler: TestHandleSignNonMemberRejected (non-member gets 403) and TestHandleSignWrongCampfireRejected (cross-campfire gets 403). Both pass. authMiddleware already enforced this via checkMembership; tests now confirm it.

payload.title: Security: path traversal in campfire_init named identity (handleInit)
payload.context:
SEVERITY: High
SOURCE: workspace-iwc2.16 security sweep

FINDING:
handleInit (cmd/cf-mcp/main.go:470) builds the identity directory path as:
  filepath.Join(home, ".campfire", "agents", name)
where `name` is user-supplied via the campfire_init MCP tool with no sanitization.

A name containing path separator components (e.g. "../../tmp/evil") escapes the
agents/ directory. filepath.Join resolves ".." components, so an attacker can
create/open directories and files anywhere the process can reach on the host
filesystem. In hosted HTTP mode (--sessions-dir), each session can call
campfire_init with a malicious name, escaping session isolation.

DONE CONDITION:
- name is validated before filepath.Join: reject any name containing "/" or "\"
  or any component that is ".." or ".".
- Test: campfire_init with name="../escape" returns an error, no directory created.
- Test: campfire_init with name="valid-agent" continues to work.

FIX:
Add a validateAgentName(name string) error check before line 470:
  if strings.ContainsAny(name, "/\\") || name == ".." || name == "." { return err }
Or use filepath.Base(name) == name as the guard (rejects any path with separators).

Close reason: Fixed: added validateAgentName() guard before filepath.Join in handleInit; rejects names with /,\,..,. or any path separator component; 4 targeted tests added, all passing (workspace-r65m)

import_history.note: Fixed: added validateAgentName() guard before filepath.Join in handleInit; rejects names with /,\,..,. or any path separator component; 4 targeted tests added, all passing (workspace-r65m)

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: handleMembership accepts peer list manipulation from any valid signer (no membership check)
payload.context:
Severity: HIGH

POST /campfire/{id}/membership updates the node's in-memory peer list and persistent peer_endpoints table based on an inbound MembershipEvent. The handler verifies the Ed25519 signature but does NOT verify that the sender is a current member of the campfire.

Attack:
- Any actor with a valid keypair can send a "join" event adding themselves or a malicious endpoint
- Any actor can send a "leave" or "evict" event removing legitimate members from the local peer list
- This enables routing manipulation: messages stop being delivered to evicted peers, or are re-routed to attacker-controlled endpoints

This is a confusion between HTTP-level authentication (valid keypair) and authorization (is this agent a campfire member with authority to announce membership changes?).

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 379-421

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: handleRekey - no test for phase-1-skipped attack (phase 2 without prior phase 1)
payload.context:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleRekey()

The rekey handler has a two-phase protocol. Phase 2 requires a cached receiver ephemeral key (stored by phase 1). If an attacker sends a phase-2 request without a prior phase-1, the handler returns 400 "no pending rekey session". This is tested in TestRekeyProtocolThreshold1 as a happy-path test, but the following cases are not tested:

Missing tests:
- Phase 2 with no prior phase 1 (storeRekeySession was never called): returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 2 with wrong SenderX25519Pub (key not in cache): returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 2 with invalid encrypted payload: decryption failure path returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 1 with invalid SenderX25519Pub hex: bad hex decode should return 400 (not tested - actually missing validation; currently just stored as-is)
- Rekey to non-member campfire (membership not found): returns 404 (not tested)
- Missing new_campfire_id or sender_x25519_pub fields: returns 400 (not tested)

The phase-1-skipped case and wrong-key case are security-critical: they test that the key cache cannot be polluted or bypassed.

Done: unit tests cover all missing error paths in handleRekey.

payload.title: Security: campfire_export tarball has no size cap and follows symlinks
payload.context:
SEVERITY: Low
SOURCE: workspace-iwc2.16 security sweep

FINDING:
handleExport (cmd/cf-mcp/main.go:1729) uses filepath.Walk to tar the session directory.
Two issues:
1. No total size cap: a session with many messages can produce a tarball that exhausts
   server memory (bytes.Buffer grows unboundedly in memory before being base64-encoded).
2. filepath.Walk follows symlinks on some platforms (behavior is OS-dependent but
   Go docs say Walk does not follow symlinks by default for directories, but DOES
   follow symlinks for files). A session directory with a crafted symlink to /etc/
   or similar could include unintended host files in the export.

DONE CONDITION:
- Export returns an error if the accumulated tarball size exceeds MaxExportSize (e.g. 50MB).
- Export skips symlinks (os.FileInfo.Mode()&os.ModeSymlink != 0).
- Test: session with >50MB of data returns -32000 "export too large".
- Test: symlink in session dir is not included in tarball.

FIX:
1. Track bytes written and abort Walk if limit exceeded.
2. In Walk callback, skip files where info.Mode()&os.ModeSymlink != 0.
   (Note: filepath.Walk resolves symlinks before calling the function, so use
   os.Lstat-based check or filepath.WalkDir with DirEntry.Type().)

Close reason: Fixed: handleExport now uses filepath.WalkDir to skip symlinks via DirEntry.Type() before resolution, and tracks bytes written to abort with -32000 error if totalWritten exceeds maxExportSize (50MB). Two new tests added: TestExport_SizeCap and TestExport_SkipsSymlinks. All 7 export tests pass.

import_history.note: Fixed: handleExport now uses filepath.WalkDir to skip symlinks via DirEntry.Type() before resolution, and tracks bytes written to abort with -32000 error if totalWritten exceeds maxExportSize (50MB). Two new tests added: TestExport_SizeCap and TestExport_SkipsSymlinks. All 7 export tests pass.

payload.title: Antipattern: campfire:* system tag strings are magic literals scattered across 6+ files
payload.context:
ANTIPATTERN FINDING from workspace-o3l.9

System tag strings are hardcoded as magic literals across multiple files with no central constants:
- "campfire:compact" — compact.go:241, store.go:377, store.go:423, read.go (comment)
- "campfire:view" — view.go (multiple places)
- "campfire:member-joined" — join.go, autojoin.go, admit.go
- "campfire:join-request" — join.go

This is an antipattern because:
1. A typo in any file silently creates a non-functional tag
2. Renaming a tag requires a grep+replace across all files
3. Tests that check these tags may use different capitalization

The role constants (RoleObserver, RoleWriter, RoleFull) in pkg/campfire/campfire.go are the correct pattern — follow that.

Fix: Add tag constants to pkg/campfire/campfire.go:
  const (
      TagCompact      = "campfire:compact"
      TagView         = "campfire:view"
      TagMemberJoined = "campfire:member-joined"
      TagJoinRequest  = "campfire:join-request"
      TagKeyDelivery  = "campfire:key-delivery"
  )

Replace all magic string literals with these constants.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: Campfire and CampfireState are near-duplicate types without a clear conversion contract
payload.context:
Pattern: Leaky abstraction / implicit coupling.

Location: pkg/campfire/campfire.go — Campfire (lines 37-44) and CampfireState (lines 53-62), plus MemberRecord (lines 65-69) which parallels Member (lines 47-51).

Campfire: has Identity *identity.Identity, Members []Member (with Role)
CampfireState: has PublicKey/PrivateKey []byte (flattened from Identity), no Members field
MemberRecord: same fields as Member

The relationship is:
- Campfire is the live/in-memory form
- CampfireState is the on-disk serialization form
- Member and MemberRecord are identical except for the type name

Issues:
1. The State() method produces CampfireState but discards the Members slice entirely — callers that need members must also write separate MemberRecord files. This implicit split contract is not documented on the type.
2. MemberRecord and Member have the same fields (PublicKey []byte, JoinedAt int64, Role string) but are separate types. Any new field added to Member must be manually mirrored to MemberRecord and vice versa, with no compile-time enforcement.
3. campfireFromState() in join.go reconstructs a Campfire from CampfireState + []MemberRecord, duplicating the construction logic.

Suggested direction: Alias MemberRecord = Member or embed one in the other with a conversion method. Document explicitly on CampfireState that it does not carry Members (filesystem transport stores those separately). Consider whether the Campfire/CampfireState split belongs in the campfire package or in the fs transport package.

Close reason: Aliased MemberRecord=Member, added CampfireState.ToCampfire(), documented the Members split contract on CampfireState, flattened Campfire.Identity into inline PublicKey/PrivateKey fields. All tests pass (pre-existing flakes ignored). Committed to work/workspace-s9c.

import_history.note: Aliased MemberRecord=Member, added CampfireState.ToCampfire(), documented the Members split contract on CampfireState, flattened Campfire.Identity into inline PublicKey/PrivateKey fields. All tests pass (pre-existing flakes ignored). Committed to work/workspace-s9c.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: handleDeliver accepts messages with spoofed Sender field — any member can impersonate another
payload.context:
handleDeliver in pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go accepts a CBOR-encoded message and stores it directly without verifying that the message inner Sender field matches the authenticated senderHex from the request signature.

Attack scenario: A campfire member M1 constructs an HTTP POST to /campfire/{id}/deliver where:
- The X-Campfire-Sender header is M1s own public key (passes auth)
- The X-Campfire-Signature is M1s valid Ed25519 signature over the body (passes auth)
- The CBOR body contains a Message where msg.Sender = M2s public key bytes

Result: The message is stored in SQLite attributed to M2 (the spoofed member). All peers who sync or poll this message will see it as if authored by M2. The msg.Signature field (if present) will fail verification against M2s key, but handleDeliver does NOT call VerifySignature() — it just calls store.AddMessage() directly.

Compare: The GitHub transport (pkg/transport/github/transport.go line 218) explicitly calls msg.VerifySignature() before storing. The HTTP transport has no equivalent check.

Impact: Any member can forge messages appearing to come from any other member (or from the campfire identity itself). This breaks the core trust model — provenance chains become meaningless. An attacker member can inject false campfire:rekey, campfire:compact, or campfire:member-joined system messages attributed to the campfire key.

Fix: In handleDeliver, verify msg.VerifySignature() before storing. Also verify that msg.Sender matches senderHex (the authenticated sender from the request headers).

Close reason: Implemented: verify msg.Sender matches authenticated sender and call msg.VerifySignature() in handleDeliver. Commit b795708, pushed.

import_history.note: Implemented: verify msg.Sender matches authenticated sender and call msg.VerifySignature() in handleDeliver. Commit b795708, pushed.

payload.title: SEC-REVIEW: replay protection nonce uniqueness skipped when transport is nil in readAndVerify
payload.context:
Reviewer: workspace-pm9m.1
Parent: workspace-pm9m

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler.go:159

Severity: major

Description:
In readAndVerify(), the nonce uniqueness check is gated on transport != nil:

    if h.transport != nil {
        if !h.transport.consumeNonce(nonce) {
            ...
        }
    }

If h.transport is nil, nonce deduplication is silently skipped. The timestamp window check (±60s) still applies, but captured requests can be replayed within the 60-second window. In production the transport is always set, but this conditional creates a confusing invariant: replay protection is not guaranteed by readAndVerify — it depends on caller state. If any test or integration code constructs a handler without a transport (which does happen in tests), replay protection is absent.

The comment on readAndVerify says "Replay protection: the signature covers timestamp+nonce+body. The server rejects requests with timestamps outside the ±60s window and nonces it has already seen" — but this is only true when transport != nil.

Done condition: Either (a) remove the nil guard and make transport non-nil a precondition of the handler, with a panic or error at construction time if transport is nil, or (b) document clearly that replay protection is best-effort when transport is nil and ensure no non-test code path creates a nil-transport handler.

Note: this does not affect production deployments where transport is always set, but it is a correctness gap in the security invariant described in the code.

Close reason: Removed nil-transport guard in readAndVerify. Nonce check is now unconditional. handler is unexported and only constructed by New() which always passes non-nil transport. Tests green.

import_history.note: Removed nil-transport guard in readAndVerify. Nonce check is now unconditional. handler is unexported and only constructed by New() which always passes non-nil transport. Tests green.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: handleJoin admits unauthenticated pubkey — JoinerPubkey not verified against X-Campfire-Sender
payload.context:
In handleJoin (pkg/transport/http/handler_join.go:263-270), after verifying the Ed25519 signature against X-Campfire-Sender, the server persists req.JoinerPubkey (from the JSON body) to the peer_endpoints table — NOT senderHex (the verified sender from the header). These can differ: a hostile joiner can set JoinerPubkey to any arbitrary hex string while signing the request as a different key. Effect: the attacker injects a fake pubkey into the peer list. For invite-only campfires, the membership check at line 130 compares senderHex against the peer list. But after joining, future membership checks for the injected pubkey will incorrectly pass. Also: AddPeer(campfireID, req.JoinerPubkey, req.JoinerEndpoint) at line 270 registers the unverified pubkey in the in-memory peer map. The fix is to use senderHex (the verified key from the header) instead of req.JoinerPubkey when persisting/registering the peer. Attack scenario: Alice joins campfire X. She sets JoinerPubkey=<Bob's pubkey> while signing with her own key. Bob (who was never admitted) now has a peer entry that passes membership checks and can send messages or participate in threshold signing under Bob's identity.

Close reason: Already fixed in workspace-17qu.4 (commit c8e68ba). Tests exist.

import_history.note: Already fixed in workspace-17qu.4 (commit c8e68ba). Tests exist.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: Duplicated auth preamble in every HTTP handler
payload.context:
Pattern: Copy-paste / SRP violation.

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go, handler_join.go, handler_sign.go, handler_rekey.go — every handler repeats the same ~15-line block.

The repeated block:
  r.Body = http.MaxBytesReader(w, r.Body, maxRequestBodySize)
  body, err := io.ReadAll(r.Body)   // (for POST handlers)
  senderHex := r.Header.Get("X-Campfire-Sender")
  sigB64 := r.Header.Get("X-Campfire-Signature")
  if senderHex == "" || sigB64 == "" { http.Error(401) }
  if err := verifyRequestSignature(...); err != nil { http.Error(401) }

handleSync and handlePoll also repeat a variant without body reading. handleJoin additionally re-does the membership check inside its invite-only block after checkMembership already ran.

Why it's problematic: Any change to auth (new header name, different error code, rate-limit hook) requires editing 5+ files. The current duplication makes it easy for one handler to accidentally omit a step; handler_join currently does NOT call checkMembership before the invite-only check, which means an unadmitted agent can hit the key provider path before the membership gate fires.

Suggested direction: Extract an authenticateRequest(w, r, campfireID) middleware function that returns (senderHex, body, ok). Wire it as a first step in route() or as an http.Handler wrapper. This centralizes auth and eliminates copy-paste.

Close reason: Already fixed: authMiddleware centralizes auth preamble. No per-handler duplication remains.

import_history.note: Already fixed: authMiddleware centralizes auth preamble. No per-handler duplication remains.

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateCampfireID - no test for transaction atomicity
payload.context:
File: pkg/store/store.go UpdateCampfireID()

UpdateCampfireID() runs a transaction that updates campfire_id across 7 tables (campfire_memberships, read_cursors, filters, peer_endpoints, threshold_shares, pending_threshold_shares, messages). There is no test for this function.

Missing tests:
- Basic rename: old ID disappears, new ID has all records
- Rename of non-existent ID: succeeds silently (no rows updated, no error)
- Messages table FK update (messages has a FK on campfire_memberships)
- Atomicity: if one table update fails mid-transaction, none should apply

This function is called during eviction/rekey. If it partially succeeds and rolls back, the campfire is in an inconsistent state (old membership still present, new state file written). No test validates the transaction behavior.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: handleSign round 2 holds global transport write lock during FROST ProcessAll/Deliver — blocks all other HTTP operations
payload.context:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler_sign.go, lines 101-128

In the round-2 branch of handleSign, the code acquires h.transport.mu.Lock() at line 101 and does NOT release it until line 128, after calling:
- ss.ProcessAll() (line 112)
- ss.Deliver() in a loop (line 120)
- ss.ProcessAll() again (line 124)
- delete(h.transport.signSessions, req.SessionID) (line 127)

h.transport.mu is the global RWMutex used for all transport state (peers, signSessions, rekeySessions, selfInfo, keyProvider, thresholdShareProvider). Holding it as a write lock during FROST cryptographic operations means every concurrent HTTP handler that acquires h.transport.mu (any lock acquisition in handleSign, handleJoin, handleRekey, AddPeer, RemovePeer, SelfInfo, etc.) is blocked until round 2 completes.

For high-latency FROST operations (the state machine processes elliptic curve arithmetic), this can block for tens to hundreds of milliseconds.

Conditions: Any concurrent HTTP request to the same Transport while a FROST round-2 is in progress.

Impact:
- All concurrent handlers (message delivery, peer polling, other sign requests) are serialized behind round-2 processing.
- For a node with multiple campfires and many peers, this becomes a significant throughput bottleneck.
- Particularly bad if a round-2 request hangs (e.g., FROST state machine stuck) — the transport is deadlocked until the request is cancelled.

The stated reason for holding the lock is to prevent pruneSignSessions from deleting the session. A narrower fix would be: acquire the lock only to extract the session pointer and release, then operate on the session under the per-session mu, then re-acquire briefly to delete the session entry.

Close reason: Narrowed global transport write lock in handleSign round 2: lock now held only briefly to extract session pointer, FROST crypto runs under per-session lock. Added TestSignRound2DoesNotBlockPeers. Committed 6f23047 on work/workspace-dq5g.

import_history.note: Narrowed global transport write lock in handleSign round 2: lock now held only briefly to extract session pointer, FROST crypto runs under per-session lock. Added TestSignRound2DoesNotBlockPeers. Committed 6f23047 on work/workspace-dq5g.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: isPeerHTTPCampfire in cmd/send.go duplicates isPeerHTTPTransportDir in pkg/transport/transport.go
payload.context:
Pattern: Copy-paste / implicit coupling.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/send.go lines 154-159 (isPeerHTTPCampfire) and pkg/transport/transport.go lines 60-68 (isPeerHTTPTransportDir).

Both functions perform the identical filesystem-stat heuristic to detect p2p-HTTP campfires: join transportDir + campfireID + ".cbor" and os.Stat the result. The pkg/transport/transport.go file even has a comment saying "isPeerHTTPTransportDir mirrors the cmd-layer isPeerHTTPCampfire check" — acknowledging the duplication explicitly but not fixing it.

This means the detection logic is in two places. If the heuristic ever changes (e.g., the state file gets renamed or the detection criterion changes), both must be updated.

Why it matters: The pkg/transport layer has the correct abstraction (ResolveType returns a typed enum). The cmd layer should call transport.ResolveType() instead of reimplementing the same stat check. The duplication also means the cmd layer bypasses the ResolveType abstraction entirely in the send path, making it inconsistent with the read/view paths.

Better pattern: Remove isPeerHTTPCampfire from send.go and replace calls with transport.ResolveType(m) == transport.TypePeerHTTP, using the Membership struct already loaded from the store.

Close reason: Implemented: removed dead isPeerHTTPCampfire from send.go — call site already uses transport.ResolveType().

payload.title: cf await command: blocks until a --future message is fulfilled
payload.context:
Add `cf await <campfire-id> <msg-id> [--timeout <duration>]` command to cmd/cf/.

**What it does:** Polls/follows a campfire for a message whose antecedents contain `{ID: msg-id, Fulfills: true}`. Returns the fulfilling message payload and exits 0. If --timeout expires, exits nonzero with empty result.

**Implementation:** Thin wrapper over the existing runFollowMode machinery in read.go. Key difference from --follow: has an exit condition (first fulfillment match), not an infinite stream.

**Done condition:**
- `cf await <campfire-id> <msg-id>` blocks and returns the fulfilling message when one arrives
- `cf await <campfire-id> <msg-id> --timeout 5s` exits nonzero after 5s if no fulfillment
- `--json` flag outputs machine-parseable JSON
- Unit test: send --future, send --fulfills, await returns the fulfiller

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: handleMembership evict — silent auth bypass when GetMembership errors
payload.context:
In pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go, the evict case of handleMembership has a conditional creator check:

  membership, err := h.store.GetMembership(campfireID)
  if err == nil && membership != nil && membership.CreatorPubkey != '' && senderHex != membership.CreatorPubkey {
      http.Error(w, 'only the campfire creator may evict members', http.StatusForbidden)
      return
  }

The check only fires when ALL four conditions are true. If GetMembership returns an error (e.g., DB failure), the evict proceeds without any creator verification. Similarly, if membership.CreatorPubkey is empty (legacy records), the check is skipped entirely.

Missing tests:
1. GetMembership returns an error → evict should be REJECTED (not silently allowed). Currently the evict proceeds.
2. CreatorPubkey is empty string → evict by any member proceeds. This is the intended backward-compat behavior, but it has no test asserting it.
3. Non-creator with a set CreatorPubkey → evict rejected with 403. (This case IS covered by existing auth tests, so only the error path is new.)

Why it matters: This is a security-critical path. If GetMembership fails transiently (DB locked, etc.), any authenticated member can evict any other member, bypassing creator-only access control. The test should confirm the handler rejects the evict on GetMembership failure rather than silently allowing it.

Close reason: Implemented: fail-closed creator check in handleMembership evict — 500 on DB error, 403 for non-creator. Added CreatorPubkey to Membership struct with migration. 4 tests.

import_history.note: Implemented: fail-closed creator check in handleMembership evict — 500 on DB error, 403 for non-creator. Added CreatorPubkey to Membership struct with migration. 4 tests.

payload.title: REVIEW-FINDING: collectSupersededIDs TOCTOU fix holds write lock during DB I/O (workspace-pm9m.5.8)
payload.context:
## Finding

Reviewer: workspace-yo8a.2

The TOCTOU fix in collectSupersededIDs (pkg/store/store.go line 829) holds the supersededMu write lock while executing a DB query (maxCompactionTimestamp calls s.db.QueryRow). 

The relevant code pattern:
  s.supersededMu.Lock()
  currentMaxTS, tsErr := s.maxCompactionTimestamp(campfireID)  // DB query under write lock
  if tsErr == nil && currentMaxTS == maxTS {
      s.supersededCache[campfireID] = ...
  }
  s.supersededMu.Unlock()

During this DB query, all concurrent ListMessages calls that reach the cache read path (s.supersededMu.RLock()) are blocked. In practice this window is short (a simple MAX() SQLite query), but holding a sync.RWMutex write lock across any I/O is an antipattern.

## Alternative approach

The correct fix would be to keep the DB query outside the lock and use a compare-and-swap pattern:
1. Query currentMaxTS BEFORE acquiring the write lock
2. Acquire write lock
3. Re-check if cache entry still absent (double-checked locking)
4. If currentMaxTS still == maxTS, store the entry
5. Release lock

However this alternative has its own subtlety: step 1 and step 3 still have a window. The current approach (DB query inside write lock) is simpler to reason about and guarantees correctness at the cost of brief lock contention.

## Severity

Minor — SQLite MAX() query is fast (microseconds). Noticeable only under high concurrent ListMessages load with frequent compactions. Not a correctness bug.

## Location

pkg/store/store.go line 829-839 (collectSupersededIDs)

Close reason: Refactored collectSupersededIDs to double-checked locking — DB query now outside write lock. Commit 0f1dcc4.

import_history.note: Refactored collectSupersededIDs to double-checked locking — DB query now outside write lock. Commit 0f1dcc4.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: handler.go violates single responsibility - one file handles 7 HTTP endpoints plus crypto plus store logic
payload.context:
pkg/transport/http/handler.go is 1057 lines and handles all of:
- Message delivery (handleDeliver)
- Sync (handleSync)
- Long-poll (handlePoll) 
- Membership notifications (handleMembership)
- Join handshake with ECDH crypto (handleJoin)
- FROST signing rounds (handleSign) 
- Two-phase rekey protocol with ECDH crypto (handleRekey)
- AES-GCM encryption/decryption helpers
- campfireStateForRekey local type
- recordToMessage conversion
- verifyRequestSignature
- HTTP request routing (route)

Each endpoint has distinct concerns: message storage, crypto key exchange, FROST signing protocol, membership state. This is a textbook god-object violation. The handler should be split into at minimum: message_handler.go, join_handler.go, sign_handler.go, rekey_handler.go.

Severity: MEDIUM - the current size makes it hard to audit individual protocol paths, and tests for one handler are intermixed with others.

Close reason: Split handler.go (1020 lines) into handler_message.go, handler_join.go, handler_sign.go, handler_rekey.go; handler.go is now 61 lines with only routing and shared types. Commit a6acf22.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: Every CLI command copy-pastes identity-load and store-open boilerplate
payload.context:
Pattern: Copy-paste / violated DRY.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/*.go — every non-trivial command (admit, compact, create, dag, disband, evict, join, leave, member, members, read, send, serve, view) contains the same 8-10 line preamble:

  agentID, err := identity.Load(IdentityPath())
  if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("loading identity: %w", err) }
  s, err := store.Open(store.StorePath(CFHome()))
  if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("opening store: %w", err) }
  defer s.Close()

This pattern appears ~14 times across the command files. The only variation is the error message prefix ("loading identity (run cf init first)" vs "loading identity").

Why it matters: Any change to initialization order (e.g., adding a migration, changing the store path logic) must be applied to every command. A command that forgets defer s.Close() leaks a DB connection — a bug that has already occurred and been fixed manually.

Better pattern: Extract a helper such as:
  func requireAgentAndStore() (*identity.Identity, *store.Store, error)

called at the top of each RunE. The defer s.Close() would need to remain in the caller but a single wrapper reduces copy-paste surface by 80%.

Close reason: Extracted openStore() and requireAgentAndStore() helpers in cmd/cf/cmd/helpers.go. Applied across all 14 command files (admit, compact, create, dag, disband, evict, join, leave, member, members, read, send, serve, view). Removed now-unused identity/store imports where possible. Build parity with HEAD maintained — pre-existing joinVia/readFields undefined errors unchanged. Committed to branch work/workspace-21c.

import_history.note: Extracted openStore() and requireAgentAndStore() helpers in cmd/cf/cmd/helpers.go. Applied across all 14 command files (admit, compact, create, dag, disband, evict, join, leave, member, members, read, send, serve, view). Removed now-unused identity/store imports where possible. Build parity with HEAD maintained — pre-existing joinVia/readFields undefined errors unchanged. Committed to branch work/workspace-21c.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: isPeerHTTPCampfire duplicated across cmd and pkg/transport layers
payload.context:
Pattern: Copy-paste / implicit coupling.

Locations:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go:154 — isPeerHTTPCampfire(transportDir, campfireID) checks os.Stat for <id>.cbor
- pkg/transport/transport.go:59 — isPeerHTTPTransportDir(transportDir, campfireID) does the same os.Stat check

The comment in transport.go even says it 'mirrors the cmd-layer isPeerHTTPCampfire check', acknowledging the duplication.

Why it's problematic: The detection heuristic (existence of <id>.cbor in transport dir) is duplicated. If the on-disk layout changes, both sites must be updated. The cmd layer calling os.Stat directly on transport internals is a leaky abstraction — the cmd layer knows the HTTP transport's storage convention.

Suggested direction: Remove isPeerHTTPCampfire from send.go. The cmd layer already calls transport.ResolveType() for routing decisions in send.go:120 and read.go. The os.Stat call in send.go:154 is dead code — it's defined but only used to check if a campfire is p2p-http, which transport.ResolveType already handles. Verify and delete the cmd-layer duplicate.

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go has zero test coverage
payload.context:
File: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go

The filesystem transport package has no test file. It is the primary transport used in init/join/leave/send/admit/disband commands and is exercised only indirectly through the cmd-layer integration tests. No unit tests exist for any of the following behaviors:

- Init(): creates directory structure, writes campfire.cbor
- WriteMember() / ListMembers() / RemoveMember(): member lifecycle
- ReadState(): decodes CampfireState from disk; error path on corrupt CBOR
- WriteMessage() / ListMessages(): message ordering (timestamp sort), skip non-.cbor files
- Remove(): removes entire transport directory
- atomicWriteCBOR(): atomic write via temp file + rename — no test for partial write or rename failure
- DefaultBaseDir(): env override via CF_TRANSPORT_DIR

ListMessages silently skips files it cannot read or decode (lines 149-155). If a file is corrupted, it is silently dropped. This is a silent breakage risk: data loss would not cause test failures.

payload.title: BUG: send.go role check runs before final tags are built — --future/--fulfills flags bypass writer check
payload.context:
Severity: major

In send.go RunE (lines 92-103), checkRoleCanSend(m.Role, sendTags) is called BEFORE the final tags slice is assembled. The --future flag appends "future" and --fulfills appends "fulfills" to tags after the check. While these two tags are not campfire:* system tags (so no security bypass today), the role check is operating on an incomplete tag list. This is a structural bug: the enforcement point does not see the same tags that will be sent.

If any future non-campfire: tag were to be restricted by role, this ordering would silently fail to block it.

Fix: perform checkRoleCanSend on the final assembled tags slice, not on sendTags.

File: cmd/cf/cmd/send.go, lines 92-103.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: Message payload treated as trusted UTF-8 and printed directly — potential terminal injection
payload.context:
Severity: LOW

Message payloads are printed directly to stdout without sanitization:
- read.go line 147: fmt.Printf("  %s\n\n", string(m.Payload))
- read.go line 214: fmt.Printf("  %s\n", string(m.Payload))
- printNATMessages: fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s\n\n", string(m.Payload))

Payloads can contain:
1. ANSI terminal escape sequences — terminal control sequences injected via message payload can overwrite screen content, move cursor, change colors, or in some terminals execute code
2. Null bytes — may truncate output in some contexts

This is a classic "terminal injection" / "ANSI injection" vulnerability. An attacker who can send a message to a campfire (even one the victim reads) can inject escape sequences that appear in the victim\s terminal.

Example: payload "\x1b[2J\x1b[H" clears the victim\s terminal. More sophisticated sequences can overwrite previously displayed text, hiding malicious activity.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/read.go lines 147, 214, 843, 858

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

Close reason: Fixed: added sanitizePayload() function that strips ESC and other control characters; applied at all three human-readable print sites

import_history.note: Fixed: added sanitizePayload() function that strips ESC and other control characters; applied at all three human-readable print sites

payload.title: PORT-CONFLICT: handler_message_params_test.go and membership_event_test.go use same ports (base+110/111/112)
payload.context:
Two new test files from Batch C use identical port offsets:

- pkg/transport/http/handler_message_params_test.go (workspace-29n): uses base+110, base+111, base+112
- pkg/transport/http/membership_event_test.go (workspace-wn1): uses base+110, base+111, base+112

Since portBase() = 19000 + (os.Getpid() % 500) is deterministic within a test run, these tests bind to the same physical ports. Tests in the same package run sequentially, and Go's SO_REUSEADDR allows port reuse after shutdown, so they pass today. However:

1. If t.Cleanup order causes a port not to be released before the next test starts, the second test's server start will fail.
2. If port reuse is flaky (TIME_WAIT on some platforms), tests become intermittently flaky.
3. The pattern makes it harder to add new tests without risk of collisions.

Fix: Renumber one set of ports. Assign handler_message_params_test.go ports base+113, base+114, base+115 (or vice versa). Audit all *_test.go files in pkg/transport/http to build a port allocation table and assign non-overlapping ranges.

Done condition: no two test functions in pkg/transport/http use the same portBase() offset. A comment at the top of http_test.go documents the allocation table.

Close reason: Implemented: Eliminated all portBase() offset conflicts in pkg/transport/http tests. Assigned each file a dedicated 20-port block (0-499). Added port allocation table comment in http_test.go. All 19 test files remapped with no overlapping offsets. Tests pass.

payload.title: REVIEW-FINDING: TestCompactBeforeSentinelIsMatchedID misses the timestamp==0 failure scenario (workspace-pm9m.5.2)
payload.context:
## Finding

Reviewer: workspace-yo8a.2

The test TestCompactBeforeSentinelIsMatchedID in cmd/cf/cmd/compact_test.go (line 315) documents the fix for the beforeTS==0 sentinel bug (workspace-pm9m.5.2) but does NOT exercise the actual failure scenario: a message with Timestamp==0 that the old code would have falsely reported as "message not found".

The test creates messages via seedMessages() which uses store.NowNano() — these have non-zero timestamps. The test verifies that --before compacts correctly with real timestamps, but never creates a message with Timestamp==0 to confirm the bug cannot recur.

## Why it matters

The fix (matchedID == "") is a one-line change that is obviously correct. But without a direct regression test for the Timestamp==0 case, a future refactor could re-introduce the sentinel bug without any test catching it.

## Suggested fix

Add a sub-test to TestCompactBeforeSentinelIsMatchedID (or a new TestCompactBeforeZeroTimestamp) that:
1. Creates a message with Timestamp=0 directly in the store
2. Calls execCompact --before that message's ID
3. Verifies it succeeds (not "message not found")

## Severity

Minor — the fix is correct; this is a test coverage gap only.

## Location

cmd/cf/cmd/compact_test.go line 315 (TestCompactBeforeSentinelIsMatchedID)

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: rekey handler allows any member to trigger campfire ID rename and evict arbitrary peers
payload.context:
handleRekey() in pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go verifies the sender is a current member of the campfire, but does not verify that the sender is the creator/owner. Any member can call POST /campfire/{id}/rekey with a phase-2 payload containing: (1) a new_campfire_id of their choosing, (2) an evicted_member_pubkey to remove any peer, (3) arbitrary encrypted key material (or a phase-1 token obtained beforehand). The handler at lines 229-241 will rename all records in the database (UpdateCampfireID) and at lines 244-246 delete the specified peer from the peer list. An evicted member who still has network access could re-evict the person who evicted them, or any other member. Combined with the fact that the handler does not verify the RekeyMessageCBOR signature against the OLD campfire key (line 263 uses rekeyMsg.VerifySignature() which validates against rekeyMsg.Sender which is attacker-controlled), a member can forge a rekey announcement and disrupt the campfire. Impact: member-level privilege escalation — any full member can evict any other member, including the creator, by sending a crafted rekey request.

Close reason: Already fixed: handleRekey has creator-only check — non-creator gets 403.

import_history.note: Already fixed: handleRekey has creator-only check — non-creator gets 403.

## Fix
Add a test that:
1. Creates a view with a negation predicate like (not (tag "foo"))
2. Adds normal messages and a campfire:view message
3. Calls runViewRead (or the underlying materialization logic directly)
4. Asserts that the output does not include any campfire:view messages

payload.title: Bug: reaper closes store but leaves session in sync.Map — use-after-reap panic
payload.context:
After reaper calls sess.Close() (which sets sess.st = nil), the session remains in m.sessions sync.Map. The next getOrCreate for the same token fast-paths through sessions.Load(), calls sess.touch(), and returns a session with st==nil. Any handler that calls store operations on that session will panic or silently fail.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: message printing logic split across printMessagesWithFields and printNATMessages
payload.context:
Two nearly identical message printing functions exist in cmd/cf/cmd/read.go:
- printMessagesWithFields() (lines 75-218): renders store.MessageRecord slices
- printNATMessages() (lines 826-860): renders message.Message slices

Both produce identical human-readable output (campfire prefix, timestamp, sender display with instance, tags line, antecedents truncated to 8 chars, payload). The duplication exists because NAT-mode messages arrive as message.Message (not yet persisted to store), but the display logic is the same.

This creates an SRP violation: the human-readable format is defined in two places. Any display change (format, field ordering, new field like campfire_id color coding) must be applied to both. 

A shared printMessageFields(cfID, sender, instance, ts, tags, antecedents, payload string) helper would centralize the format. Severity: MEDIUM - display bugs will be inconsistent across read modes.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: evictThresholdN counts remainingPeers incorrectly for new DKG participant count
payload.context:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, evictThresholdN (line 257)

n := uint(len(remainingPeers))

remainingPeers is built from store peer_endpoints excluding the evicted member (line 99-104). However, the creator/self is included in remainingPeers if they have a stored peer endpoint, but excluded from delivery (line 326: skips agentID.PublicKeyHex()). 

The DKG is run with n participants and participant IDs [1..n]. The creator is assigned participant 1 (line 314). The remaining non-self peers get IDs starting at 2 (line 316-321).

Problem: if the creator also has a stored peer_endpoint entry (which is stored during join/serve), then len(remainingPeers) includes the creator. The DKG runs for n participants. But then peerToParticipantID skips agentID.PublicKeyHex() when incrementing nextPID (line 317-321), so peers get IDs 2..n. The creator gets ID 1. But the DKG was run with n participants — and if the creator IS in remainingPeers, those n slots are correctly filled.

If the creator is NOT in remainingPeers (no self peer_endpoint), n would be the count of other peers only. The creator is not counted, so the DKG runs for n+1 participants (n other peers + 1 creator) but n was set to just the count of other peers. In this case the DKG runs with fewer participants than expected: participantIDs = [1..n] but there are actually n+1 members.

Actually: n = len(remainingPeers) where remainingPeers includes all non-evicted peers from peer_endpoints. The creator may or may not be in peer_endpoints. If the creator has no endpoint stored (did not call serve or join with --listen), they would not appear in remainingPeers. The DKG would then run with n participants (only the other members), assigning IDs 1..n. Creator gets ID 1, others get 2..n. But the creator is the n+1-th participant that was NOT counted in n.

This means the DKG is run with n participants instead of n+1, producing shares for n people, but the creator is also using participant ID 1. The creator share (dkgResults[1]) is valid for a DKG of n participants, but the distributed shares go to n-1 other peers (IDs 2..n), leaving 1 uncounted.

Net effect: with t-of-n threshold and creator not in peer_endpoints, the DKG produces a (t-1)-of-(n-1) system missing the creator from the participant count. The subsequent signing would fail because the group public key does not match the expected members.

Close reason: Fixed: evictThresholdN now includes self in participant count (worktree-agent-a699bc1e)

import_history.note: Fixed: evictThresholdN now includes self in participant count (worktree-agent-a699bc1e)

payload.title: Security: predicate evaluator has no recursion depth limit — stack overflow possible
payload.context:
SECURITY FINDING from workspace-o3l.6

The predicate evaluator in pkg/predicate/predicate.go uses mutual recursion (eval → eval) with no depth limit. A malformed predicate like (and (and (and ... 10000 deep ...))) will overflow the Go stack and crash the process.

Affected: pkg/predicate/predicate.go eval() function
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/predicate/predicate.go

Fix: Add a depth counter to parseExpr and eval. Return an error at Parse time if depth > 50 (reasonable max for real predicates).

Done condition: Parse() returns an error for predicates nested deeper than a configurable limit. Tests verify the limit is enforced.

Close reason: Fixed: MaxDepth=64 with ErrDepthExceeded in predicate evaluator. Commit e64a658.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: ListMessages RespectCompaction filters by afterTimestamp but compaction events may predate cursor
payload.context:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go, ListMessages (line 314-388)

When RespectCompaction=true, ListMessages:
1. Fetches messages WHERE timestamp > afterTimestamp (line 321-323)
2. Fetches ALL compaction events via collectSupersededIDs (line 371-377) which calls ListCompactionEvents

ListCompactionEvents has NO afterTimestamp filter -- it fetches ALL campfire:compact events regardless of when they were created (line 431-459).

This creates an asymmetry: if a compaction event was created BEFORE the afterTimestamp cursor, it will not appear in the filtered msgs list (correct -- its timestamp is too old). But it WILL appear in the superseded set (via collectSupersededIDs), so messages it supersedes would be filtered out of the result.

Scenario: 
- Messages M1, M2 created at T=100, T=200
- Compaction event C1 created at T=300, superseding M1 and M2
- User reads with afterTimestamp=400 (has already seen C1 but wants any NEW messages)
- New message M3 created at T=500

ListMessages(campfireID, 400, RespectCompaction=true) would:
- Fetch msgs where timestamp > 400: only M3
- Collect superseded from ALL compaction events: {M1.ID, M2.ID} from C1
- Filter: M3 is not in superseded, so M3 is returned. Correct.

This scenario is fine. But consider:
- User reads with afterTimestamp=250 (cursor between M2 and C1)
- Compaction C1 exists at T=300

ListMessages(campfireID, 250, RespectCompaction=true):
- Fetch msgs where timestamp > 250: C1 (at T=300) and M3 (at T=500)
- collectSupersededIDs includes M1.ID and M2.ID (from C1)
- C1 is included (isCompactionEvent), M3 is included
- M1 and M2 were already before the cursor so not in result anyway

Still fine. The bug scenario:
- Cursor at T=350 (past C1)
- NEW compaction C2 at T=450 superseding M3 (which was at T=500... wait M3 would not be superseded yet)

Let me reconsider: the real edge case is when multiple compaction events exist and the cursor is between them. The superseded set from ALL compaction events includes messages superseded by OLDER compactions that were before the cursor. When the user fetches messages AFTER the cursor (expecting to see new messages), the compaction events before the cursor still affect the filter.

This is actually CORRECT behavior -- you want to see the current non-superseded state even for new messages. The bug is different: collectSupersededIDs could be very expensive for campfires with many compaction events because it fetches and parses ALL compaction payloads on every ListMessages call with RespectCompaction. There is no indexing or caching of the superseded set. For a campfire with many compaction events and thousands of superseded IDs, this is an O(events * ids) operation on every read.

Close reason: Fixed: collectSupersededIDs now caches the superseded-ID set per campfire keyed by max compaction timestamp. Cache miss only on new compaction events. Cross-campfire queries fall through uncached. Committed in befb820.

import_history.note: Fixed: collectSupersededIDs now caches the superseded-ID set per campfire keyed by max compaction timestamp. Cache miss only on new compaction events. Cross-campfire queries fall through uncached. Committed in befb820.

payload.title: Bug: handleAwait and handleExport open second SQLite connection in session mode
payload.context:
handleAwait (line 1082) and handleExport (line 1707) both call store.Open(s.storePath()). In session mode, the session already holds an open store (sess.st). This creates a second SQLite connection to the same file. 

For handleExport: the session's in-flight WAL may not be checkpointed, so the exported store.db could be missing recent messages.

For handleAwait: a second connection is unnecessary overhead and risks SQLite locking contention.

Fix: the session's store should be passed through to these handlers instead of re-opening. In session mode, sess.st is already open and valid.

Files: cmd/cf-mcp/main.go handleAwait() line 1082-1086, handleExport() line 1707-1711.

Done condition: in session mode, handleAwait and handleExport use the session's open store (sess.st), not a newly opened connection. No second open/close cycle on the same db file.

Source: workspace-iwc2.15 review

Close reason: Fix already landed by swarm (commit 720ffe6 + earlier). handleAwait uses s.st when non-nil (avoids second SQLite connection); handleExport skips open/close when s.st is non-nil (WAL already managed by session store). server.st field added to struct; Session.server() passes st through. All export and session tests pass.

import_history.note: Fix already landed by swarm (commit 720ffe6 + earlier). handleAwait uses s.st when non-nil (avoids second SQLite connection); handleExport skips open/close when s.st is non-nil (WAL already managed by session store). server.st field added to struct; Session.server() passes st through. All export and session tests pass.
