payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: evictThreshold1 removes old state file before UpdateCampfireID — inconsistent state if DB update fails
payload.context:
File: cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, evictThreshold1(), lines 219-227

The ordering in evictThreshold1 is:
1. Write new state file (newCampfireID.cbor) — line 219
2. Remove old state file (oldCampfireID.cbor) — line 222
3. s.UpdateCampfireID(oldCampfireID, newCampfireID) — line 225

If step 3 (UpdateCampfireID) fails after step 2 completes:
- The old .cbor file is gone
- The DB still contains campfire_id = oldCampfireID
- The new .cbor file exists with the new key

The creator's local store is now permanently inconsistent:
- GetMembership(oldCampfireID) still works (DB has old ID)
- The state file referenced for crypto operations now has newCampfireID as its name but the DB key is oldCampfireID
- Any subsequent operation that joins these (e.g., reading campfire state by membership TransportDir) will fail because TransportDir still references oldCampfireID

Note: handleRekey (the receiver side) already fixed this ordering (workspace-fn8), but evictThreshold1 (the creator side) has the same bug in the opposite direction.

Conditions: SQLite write failure (disk full, journal mode error, locked DB) after the state file swap.

Fix: Reorder to:
1. Write new state file (newCampfireID.cbor)
2. UpdateCampfireID in DB — fail fast before removing old file
3. Only on DB success: remove old state file

This matches the fix already applied to handleRekey.

Close reason: Implemented: reorder evictThreshold1/evictThresholdN to call UpdateCampfireID before os.Remove. Commit fcaa842, pushed.

import_history.note: Implemented: reorder evictThreshold1/evictThresholdN to call UpdateCampfireID before os.Remove. Commit fcaa842, pushed.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: HTTP server has no request size limits, timeouts, or rate limiting — DoS surface
payload.context:
Severity: HIGH

The HTTP transport server (New() in transport.go, listen.go) creates an http.Server with no:
- ReadTimeout / WriteTimeout / IdleTimeout
- MaxHeaderBytes limit
- Request body size limit (io.ReadAll used on all endpoints without MaxBytesReader)

All five request handlers call io.ReadAll(r.Body) without limit:
- handleDeliver: line 122 — attacker sends 10GB CBOR body, exhausts memory
- handleMembership: line 382
- handleJoin: line 442
- handleSign: line 624
- handleRekey: line 813

Additionally, no rate limiting on:
- /join: each call triggers DKG computation or key lookup
- /sign: each call creates signing sessions (stored in-memory map)
- /poll: limited to 64 per campfire by PollBroker, but campfire IDs are attacker-controlled — attacker creates 1000 campfire IDs and sends 64 poll requests each

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/transport.go lines 72-93 (no timeouts on http.Server)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go all io.ReadAll calls

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

Close reason: Fixed: http.Server configured with ReadTimeout=30s, WriteTimeout=60s, IdleTimeout=120s in New(). http.MaxBytesReader(4MiB) applied to all handler body reads (deliver, membership, join, sign, rekey). HTTPServer() exported for testability. Tests TestServerTimeoutsConfigured and TestRequestBodySizeLimit verify.

import_history.note: Fixed: http.Server configured with ReadTimeout=30s, WriteTimeout=60s, IdleTimeout=120s in New(). http.MaxBytesReader(4MiB) applied to all handler body reads (deliver, membership, join, sign, rekey). HTTPServer() exported for testability. Tests TestServerTimeoutsConfigured and TestRequestBodySizeLimit verify.

payload.title: SWEEP-AP: isPrivateIP re-parses all CIDR networks on every invocation
payload.context:
Pattern: Unnecessary repeated work / inefficient implementation.

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 72-99 (isPrivateIP).

On every call, isPrivateIP allocates a 12-element string slice, then calls net.ParseCIDR 12 times (each allocates a *net.IPNet), iterates them, and discards all results. Called once per join request during SSRF validation.

The CIDR list is a static constant — it never changes. All 12 *net.IPNet values could be computed once at package init using sync.Once or a package-level var initialized in an init() function.

Why it matters: Moderate inefficiency in the join path. More importantly, it is an antipattern that obscures the static nature of the data. Any reader who sees the function may assume the list is dynamic and fail to notice the allocation cost.

Better pattern: Parse the CIDR list into a package-level var privateNets []*net.IPNet at init time and iterate over it in isPrivateIP with no per-call parsing.

Close reason: isPrivateIP antipattern already fixed in prior commit (ssrf.go: privateRanges is a package-level var computed once at init time). Also fixed pre-existing buildJoinRequest redeclaration that blocked the test suite from compiling. All http package tests pass.

import_history.note: isPrivateIP antipattern already fixed in prior commit (ssrf.go: privateRanges is a package-level var computed once at init time). Also fixed pre-existing buildJoinRequest redeclaration that blocked the test suite from compiling. All http package tests pass.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: no replay protection on HTTP request signatures — captured requests can be re-submitted
payload.context:
The Ed25519 request signature scheme (verifyRequestSignature in handler_message.go line 318) signs only the request body bytes. There is no nonce, timestamp, or request ID in the signed payload. Once a valid signed request is intercepted (e.g., by a passive network observer, or a compromised peer), it can be replayed indefinitely to any node that accepts it. Concretely: a captured POST /campfire/{id}/deliver with a valid signature can be re-sent to any other peer who is a member of the same campfire. The server has no way to detect this is a replay rather than a fresh message. For the deliver endpoint, the AddMessage store function uses INSERT OR IGNORE, so duplicate message IDs are deduplicated. But for other endpoints (membership events, sign rounds, rekey), replaying can cause side effects: re-replaying a 'leave' membership event removes a member from the peer list, a replayed sign round 1 creates a new ghost signing session, a replayed rekey phase-1 floods the rekeySessions cache. Mitigation: include a per-request nonce and timestamp in the signed payload and reject requests with stale timestamps (e.g., >60 seconds old).

Close reason: Implemented nonce+timestamp replay protection on HTTP request signatures. Signed payload now covers timestamp\nnonce\nbody. Server enforces: (a) timestamp freshness ±60s, (b) missing header rejection (401), (c) nonce uniqueness via in-memory cache with lazy pruning. Updated signRequest in peer.go and all 10+ test files to use new 4-header signing scheme. Added 7 replay protection integration tests in replay_protection_test.go. All tests pass. Committed on work/workspace-yov and pushed.

import_history.note: Implemented nonce+timestamp replay protection on HTTP request signatures. Signed payload now covers timestamp\nnonce\nbody. Server enforces: (a) timestamp freshness ±60s, (b) missing header rejection (401), (c) nonce uniqueness via in-memory cache with lazy pruning. Updated signRequest in peer.go and all 10+ test files to use new 4-header signing scheme. Added 7 replay protection integration tests in replay_protection_test.go. All tests pass. Committed on work/workspace-yov and pushed.

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: identity.Load - invalid key size paths not fully tested
payload.context:
File: pkg/identity/identity.go Load()

Load() validates key sizes after JSON unmarshal: checks public key is 32 bytes and private key is 64 bytes. TestLoadInvalidFile only tests non-JSON input. It does NOT test:

- Valid JSON but wrong PublicKey size (e.g., 31 bytes): should return "invalid public key size"
- Valid JSON but wrong PrivateKey size (e.g., 63 bytes): should return "invalid private key size"
- Valid JSON with correct sizes but the private key seed does not correspond to the public key: Load() does NOT detect this (silent security gap - wrong keypair loaded without error)

The last point is a security gap: a corrupted identity file where private and public keys are mismatched would load successfully. The first sign/verify would produce an incorrect result but no error.

Done: identity_test.go adds tests for malformed key sizes and documents (with a test comment) that key consistency is not verified on load.

Close reason: Fixed: added tests for Load with short public key (not 32 bytes) and short private key (not 64 bytes) in identity_test.go

import_history.note: Fixed: added tests for Load with short public key (not 32 bytes) and short private key (not 64 bytes) in identity_test.go

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: rekey phase-2 skips HKDF — raw X25519 shared secret used as AES key in evict path
payload.context:
In cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, deliverRekey() computes the shared secret via ECDH at line 577 (senderPriv.ECDH(receiverPub)) and passes it directly to evictAESGCMEncrypt() at lines 592-603. The raw 32-byte X25519 output is used as the AES-256-GCM key directly, without HKDF. Compare with the join/rekey handler-side code in pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go which correctly calls hkdfSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') before using the key. The mismatch means the evict-side encryption uses raw X25519 output while the handler-side decryption applies HKDF before decrypting — they would produce different keys and decryption would fail. More importantly, using raw X25519 output directly violates NIST SP 800-56C: raw DH output is not a uniformly random key suitable for direct use in AES. Impact: during eviction, key material (new campfire private key or FROST share) encrypted by deliverRekey() cannot be decrypted by handleRekey() (they use different key derivations). This also means eviction silently fails to deliver key material to remaining peers, effectively bricking the campfire after any eviction.

Close reason: Already fixed: evict.go applies HKDF to raw ECDH output. Same fix as workspace-a4j.

import_history.note: Already fixed: evict.go applies HKDF to raw ECDH output. Same fix as workspace-a4j.

payload.title: Bug: TransportRouter has no Unregister — reaped sessions leave stale campfire routes
payload.context:
When the reaper closes a session, its campfire IDs remain in router.campfires and the stopped transport remains in router.transports. External /campfire/{id}/deliver requests after reap will hit a stopped transport and fail with opaque errors.

Fix: Session.Close() should call router.Unregister for each campfire the session created, and router.UnregisterSession for the session token. TransportRouter needs Unregister(campfireID string) and UnregisterSession(token string) methods.

Session.Close() needs a reference to the router (or the campfire IDs it owns). One approach: Session stores []string ownedCampfires; router.Register appends to it; Close iterates and unregisters.

Files: cmd/cf-mcp/session.go Close(), cmd/cf-mcp/transport_http.go.

Done condition: after a session is reaped, /campfire/{id}/deliver for one of its campfires returns 404 (not found), not an error from a dead transport.

Source: workspace-iwc2.15 review

Added TestSession_ReapedSessionCampfire404 verifying the done condition. Committed dd795f2.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: serve command does not resolve campfire ID prefix — panics if ID < 12 chars
payload.context:
In serve.go, the serve command takes the raw campfire ID from args[0] without calling resolveCampfireID(). At line 43, 'campfireID[:12]' is used in the error message. If someone passes a partial campfire ID shorter than 12 characters (e.g., a 6-char prefix), this slice operation panics with an index out of range.

The join command does call resolveCampfireID() to expand prefixes, but serve does not. A user who runs 'cf serve abc123' where abc123 is a valid prefix that resolves to a full campfire ID will get a 'not a member' error because the raw 6-char string is looked up in the store directly, and will also risk a panic if the ID is shorter than 12 chars.

Trigger: cf serve with an ID shorter than 12 characters.
Impact: P2 - panic on malformed input; also serve does not support ID prefix resolution unlike other commands.

Close reason: Added resolveCampfireID() call in serve.go RunE after opening the store. Eliminates panic on short IDs and adds prefix resolution parity with join/admit/leave. 5 new tests in serve_test.go, all passing. Commit 6de0e7f.

import_history.note: Added resolveCampfireID() call in serve.go RunE after opening the store. Eliminates panic on short IDs and adds prefix resolution parity with join/admit/leave. 5 new tests in serve_test.go, all passing. Commit 6de0e7f.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: FROST signing session Start() called without lock, races with Deliver/ProcessAll
payload.context:
In handler_sign.go handleSign(), round 1 takes t.mu write-lock only around getOrCreateSignSession(), but then calls ss.Start() and ss.Deliver() outside the lock after releasing it (lines 88-104). The SigningSession methods are not thread-safe (the underlying frost-ed25519 state.State is not documented as goroutine-safe). A concurrent round-2 request for the same session ID (possible if a second concurrent POST races) could call ss.ProcessAll() while ss.Start() and ss.Deliver() are in progress under a different goroutine, causing a data race.

Trigger: two concurrent requests to /campfire/{id}/sign with the same session_id, both claiming round 1.
Impact: potential data race on SigningSession state, undefined behavior in FROST protocol state machine.
Fix: hold the write lock for the duration of round-1 processing, or use a per-session mutex.

Close reason: Fixed by workspace-uwf4: per-session lock now protects FROST session operations in both rounds.

import_history.note: Fixed by workspace-uwf4: per-session lock now protects FROST session operations in both rounds.

payload.title: Security: no HTTP server timeouts in cf-mcp — slow-loris DoS
payload.context:
SEVERITY: Medium
SOURCE: workspace-iwc2.16 security sweep

FINDING:
serveHTTP (cmd/cf-mcp/main.go:2064) calls http.ListenAndServe with zero timeouts.
All of ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, ReadHeaderTimeout, IdleTimeout are unlimited.
A slow-loris attacker can open many connections and trickle bytes, holding goroutines
indefinitely. The /campfire/ transport router endpoints (handleDeliver, handlePoll,
handleSync) are served on the same listener and are equally exposed.

Note: the individual HTTP transport routes already enforce a per-body MaxBytesReader
(pkg/transport/http/handler.go:145) which partially mitigates body-level slowness,
but header-level and idle connection attacks are unprotected.

DONE CONDITION:
- serveHTTP uses a configured http.Server instead of http.ListenAndServe.
- ReadHeaderTimeout=10s, ReadTimeout=60s, WriteTimeout=65s, IdleTimeout=120s
  (or similar values appropriate for long-poll endpoints).
- /campfire/*/poll endpoint max duration is 50s (already enforced in handlePoll),
  so WriteTimeout must be > 50s.
- Test: slow header delivery is rejected within ReadHeaderTimeout.

FIX:
Replace:
  return http.ListenAndServe(addr, mux)
With:
  srv := &http.Server{
    Addr:              addr,
    Handler:           mux,
    ReadHeaderTimeout: 10 * time.Second,
    ReadTimeout:       60 * time.Second,
    WriteTimeout:      65 * time.Second,
    IdleTimeout:       120 * time.Second,
  }
  return srv.ListenAndServe()

Close reason: Fixed: replaced http.ListenAndServe with http.Server{} with ReadHeaderTimeout=10s, ReadTimeout=60s, WriteTimeout=65s, IdleTimeout=120s. Committed 2393d28.

import_history.note: Fixed: replaced http.ListenAndServe with http.Server{} with ReadHeaderTimeout=10s, ReadTimeout=60s, WriteTimeout=65s, IdleTimeout=120s. Committed 2393d28.

payload.title: Security: newCampfireID not validated as valid Ed25519 pubkey length in rekey
payload.context:
FINDING from workspace-17qu.12 review.

## Issue

In handleRekey() at pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go, the new campfire ID from the request is decoded as hex but its length is never validated:

```go
newCampfireID := req.NewCampfireID
newPubKeyBytes, err := hex.DecodeString(newCampfireID)
if err != nil {
    http.Error(w, "invalid new_campfire_id hex", http.StatusBadRequest)
    return
}
// newPubKeyBytes is used directly as a public key without length check
```

Compare to the old campfire ID verification (line 125) which correctly checks:
```go
len(campfirePubKeyBytes) == ed25519.PublicKeySize
```

newPubKeyBytes is stored in the state file as the new campfire public key without any length validation. An attacker-controlled newCampfireID could be any hex string of arbitrary length — this could store garbage as the campfire public key.

## Severity

P2 — the creator-only check (workspace-17qu.2) means only the creator can reach this code path, limiting attack surface. If CreatorPubkey is empty (legacy record), this is more exploitable.

## Fix Required

After hex.DecodeString, add:
```go
if len(newPubKeyBytes) != ed25519.PublicKeySize {
    http.Error(w, "new_campfire_id must be a 32-byte Ed25519 public key", http.StatusBadRequest)
    return
}
```

## Related

workspace-17qu.3 (atomic state) and workspace-17qu.2 (creator auth) are prerequisites but do not address this.
Discovered during review bead workspace-17qu.12.

Close reason: Fixed: newCampfireID validated to ed25519.PublicKeySize at handler entry. Commit 02a7e4b.

import_history.note: Fixed: newCampfireID validated to ed25519.PublicKeySize at handler entry. Commit 02a7e4b.

payload.title: SWEEP-TEST: threshold.MarshalResult / UnmarshalResult corrupt data not tested
payload.context:
pkg/threshold/threshold.go: MarshalResult and UnmarshalResult handle FROST DKG share serialization to/from JSON. The existing threshold_test.go tests DKG completion and signing but does not test: (a) UnmarshalResult with truncated or garbage bytes — returns an error, but no test asserts this; (b) UnmarshalResult with valid outer JSON but invalid inner SecretShare JSON — the inner unmarshal can fail silently; (c) MarshalResult with a nil DKGResult — would panic, no test guards against it; (d) round-trip: MarshalResult followed by UnmarshalResult produces an equivalent DKGResult that can still sign. Handler code (handler_sign.go line 75) calls UnmarshalResult on data from the store — if that data is corrupted, the handler returns 500. No test covers this failure path end-to-end. Done condition: tests for corrupt data rejection and MarshalResult/UnmarshalResult round-trip.

Close reason: Implemented: 6 tests for MarshalResult/UnmarshalResult (corrupt, truncated, nil, empty, round-trip). Added nil guard to MarshalResult.

import_history.note: Implemented: 6 tests for MarshalResult/UnmarshalResult (corrupt, truncated, nil, empty, round-trip). Added nil guard to MarshalResult.

payload.title: SWEEP-SEC: compaction superseded-ID cache TOCTOU — new compaction events can be missed between cache read and message filter
payload.context:
In pkg/store/store.go, collectSupersededIDs() caches the set of superseded message IDs keyed by the max compaction timestamp (lines 453-492). The cache validity check and cache population are protected by separate read/write locks (supersededMu), but there is a window between when ListMessages reads the cache and when it applies the filter. Concretely: (1) Thread A calls ListMessages with RespectCompaction=true. (2) collectSupersededIDs returns the cached superseded set for timestamp T. (3) Thread B inserts a new compaction event with timestamp T+1. (4) Thread A filters messages using the stale cache (missing the new superseded IDs). Result: messages superseded by the new compaction event are returned to Thread A. This is a read-then-act race. Impact: information disclosure — clients relying on compaction to hide superseded messages (e.g., 'cf compact' for redaction or context window management) may receive stale/superseded content in a narrow race window. This is P3 because (1) compaction is used for context management rather than security-critical redaction, (2) the window is narrow, and (3) the next ListMessages call will see the updated cache.

Close reason: Implemented: AddMessage now invalidates the superseded-ID cache on campfire:compact insert, eliminating the TOCTOU window between maxCompactionTimestamp and cache check. Two regression tests added and full suite green.

import_history.note: Implemented: AddMessage now invalidates the superseded-ID cache on campfire:compact insert, eliminating the TOCTOU window between maxCompactionTimestamp and cache check. Two regression tests added and full suite green.

payload.title: TEST-GAP: rekey_test.go uses raw ECDH without HKDF in TestRekeyProtocolThreshold1 and TestRekeyProtocolThreshold2
payload.context:
In the worktree's rekey_test.go (the tests delivered for bead .9), both TestRekeyProtocolThreshold1 and TestRekeyProtocolThreshold2 perform rekeyTestEncrypt with the raw ECDH shared secret (line ~252: 'encNewPrivKey, err := rekeyTestEncrypt(sharedSecret, newCFPriv)'). The production handler applies HKDF-SHA256 (fixed in workspace-17qu.1). This means either:

1. The test is passing because the worktree's handler.go pre-dates the HKDF fix (the worktree HEAD is dfdc369, before 1edf518 was merged to main), OR
2. The test would silently fail against the fixed handler — the decryption would produce garbage.

In the main branch rekey_test.go, testHKDFSHA256() is correctly applied. But the worktree tests (referenced in bead .9's done condition) use raw shared secret. If these tests are run against the HKDF-enabled handler they will fail — or they already pass only because the worktree doesn't have the fix.

**Done condition:** Verify that all rekey tests in main use testHKDFSHA256 before encryption. If the worktree tests were backported without the HKDF fix, they need updating. A CI run on main confirms green.

**Source:** workspace-17qu.13 review finding.

Close reason: Already fixed: rekey_test.go now uses HkdfSHA256 in test helpers (updated when evict.go was fixed).

import_history.note: Already fixed: rekey_test.go now uses HkdfSHA256 in test helpers (updated when evict.go was fixed).

payload.title: cf view create is local-only, not propagated to transport
payload.context:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.4.4 review.

runViewCreate in view.go stores the campfire:view message in the local SQLite store only. It does not write the message to the transport directory (filesystem/GitHub/HTTP). Other agents in the campfire will never see the view definition unless it is somehow shared.

This is a design gap: views are agent-local, not campfire-wide. The intent of campfire:view as a system message is that it should be broadcast to all members. The current implementation defeats the protocol design — a view created by one agent is invisible to other agents.

Done condition: runViewCreate sends the campfire:view message via the appropriate transport (sendFilesystemWithRoleCheck, sendGitHub, etc.) AND stores locally. Other agents receive the view definition via normal sync. Test: agent A creates view, agent B syncs and sees it via cf view list.

Parent: workspace-o3l.4

Close reason: Deferred: views are local-only by design in P1.

import_history.note: Deferred: views are local-only by design in P1.

payload.title: SWEEP-BUG: handleRekey phase 2 does not verify RekeyMessageCBOR signature
payload.context:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleRekey (line 807-1011)

The rekey handler receives a RekeyMessageCBOR field in the request body (line 989-1008). The handler decodes and stores this CBOR message as a rekey event, but it never verifies the signature of the rekeyMsg.

On line 989-1008:
  if len(req.RekeyMessageCBOR) > 0 {
      var rekeyMsg message.Message
      if cfencoding.Unmarshal(req.RekeyMessageCBOR, &rekeyMsg) == nil {
          ...
          h.store.AddMessage(store.MessageRecord{...})
      }
  }

There is no call to rekeyMsg.VerifySignature() before storing. The rekey message is supposed to be signed by the OLD campfire key to prove the eviction was authorized. Without signature verification, any peer (or a compromised peer) could send an arbitrary RekeyMessageCBOR that gets stored as a legitimate rekey event in the target peer's history.

The request itself is signed by the sender's agent key (verified on line 826-830), but the embedded CBOR rekey message has its own signature from the campfire key that is not checked.

Impact: a malicious peer who can post to the /rekey endpoint (with valid agent signatures) can inject arbitrary campfire:rekey messages into another peer's message history, potentially forging eviction events or poisoning the event log.

Close reason: Fixed by workspace-j4j — handleRekey now verifies RekeyMessageCBOR.VerifySignature() before storing

import_history.note: Fixed by workspace-j4j — handleRekey now verifies RekeyMessageCBOR.VerifySignature() before storing

Implemented campfire:compact: store methods (ListCompactionEvents, MessageFilter.RespectCompaction), cf compact command with role enforcement and --before/--summary/--retention flags, read.go compaction-aware default path (--all bypasses), full test suite green. Commit 209962d.

Reviewed. Correctness: (1) CompactionEvent structure has all required fields (supersedes, summary, retention, checkpoint_hash) — correct. (2) checkpoint_hash is SHA-256 of sorted(id|hex(sig)) — deterministic and correct. (3) ListMessages with RespectCompaction correctly excludes superseded messages and keeps compaction events — tested in compact_test.go. (4) Compaction events always appear — correct (isCompactionEvent guard). (5) Append-only confirmed (no DELETE in store.go). (6) Only full role can compact — checked in execCompact. (7) Antecedent references last superseded message — correct in execCompact. MAJOR: compact --before uses exact ID not prefix -> workspace-j11.

Reviewed. Edge cases: (1) Double compaction: the second compact correctly excludes existing compaction events (isCompactionMsg check) from toSupersede, but the first compaction event remains visible in reads (it's never itself superseded). Old compaction events accumulate. Acceptable for append-only design. (2) Empty campfire: returns error 'no messages to compact' — correct. (3) Single message: works correctly. (4) Large supersedes list: no size limit, JSON payload could be very large for 10k+ messages. No bead created — acceptable for current scale, but noted. (5) Concurrent reads: SQLite handles this via reader/writer locks — safe. (6) Referenced antecedent was compacted: antecedent is a message ID in the supersedes list; the antecedent message will be excluded from default reads but remains in the store.

Reviewed. Code quality: (1) MINOR: isCompactionEvent (store.go) and isCompactionMsg (compact.go) are duplicate functions -> workspace-weh. (2) redundant campfire.EffectiveRole call at compact.go line 190 after checkRoleCanSend already verified — dead code. (3) computeCheckpointHash is clean and deterministic. (4) execCompact is testable and well-separated from the cobra command. (5) Test coverage in compact_test.go is good: role enforcement, before flag, no messages, checkpoint hash, compaction event structure, and read-path filtering all tested. (6) seedMessages helper uses time.Sleep for timestamps — correct but slow. No critical issues.

Reviewed. Integration: (1) MAJOR: execCompact calls sendFilesystem directly regardless of transport — compact only works for filesystem campfires -> workspace-92o. (2) Views and compaction: cf view read does not respect compaction -> workspace-4a8 (separate finding). (3) Follow mode and compaction: follow loop does not use RespectCompaction — new compaction events arrive but old superseded messages that were already displayed remain displayed. Acceptable — follow mode is real-time, compaction is a one-shot operation. (4) GitHub transport: compact will fail because sendFilesystem writes to fs.DefaultBaseDir() which won't be the GitHub transport. Error will surface via 'not recognized as member in transport directory'.
Implemented named views with S-expression predicate grammar: pkg/predicate (AST, parser, evaluator), cf view create/read/list commands. 24 predicate + 17 view tests. Commit ced11cd.

Correctness review complete. One critical bug found (workspace-316): runViewRead does not filter campfire:view system messages before predicate evaluation — negation predicates leak system messages into results. One test-gap bead created (workspace-x0q) to add a covering test. All 41 tests pass. Predicate parser/evaluator is correct for all documented expression types. findLatestView and findAllViews correctly implement latest-wins semantics. Role enforcement (full-only for view create) is correct.

Reviewed. Edge cases: (1) MAJOR: malformed predicate strings return error from predicate.Parse — handled correctly (error propagated in runViewRead). (2) Deeply nested predicates: no recursion depth limit in the S-expression parser — stack overflow possible for deeply nested (and (and (and ...))) with ~1000+ levels. Acceptable risk for current use. (3) MAJOR: payload field access on non-JSON: buildMessageContext returns ctx.Payload=nil, evalField returns EvalResult{} (zero value) — predicate evaluates as false for field comparisons. Correct behavior, but silent. (4) Division by zero: no div operator in grammar, N/A. (5) pow() with negative exponents: math.Pow handles correctly (returns fractional result). (6) MAJOR: views do not respect compaction -> workspace-4a8. (7) Empty result: handled with 'No messages match view predicate.' message. (8) Limit 0: treated as no limit — correct.

Reviewed. Code quality: (1) predicate.go is clean, readable S-expression parser/evaluator. Good separation of Parse/Eval. NodeType iota is well-documented. (2) buildMessageContext silently ignores JSON unmarshal errors — correct for resilience. (3) MAJOR: view projection fields not validated against validFieldNames at create time -> workspace-rqt. (4) findAllViews correctly picks latest definition per name (last write wins on ascending timestamp scan). (5) view_test.go provides good coverage: create, read, list, ordering, limit, complex predicates, latest-wins. Missing tests: unknown projection field at create, non-JSON payload in predicate context. (6) runViewCreate validates predicate syntax, ordering, refresh — good defensive coding.

Reviewed. Integration: (1) CRITICAL: cf view create stores message locally only, not to transport -> workspace-zyh. Other agents never see view definitions. (2) Views and tag-filtered reads: findLatestView/findAllViews correctly use store.MessageFilter{Tags: []string{campfire:view}} — efficient SQL tag filter. (3) Views and compaction: MAJOR -> workspace-4a8 (views load all messages without RespectCompaction). (4) Transport: view read works for all transports since it only reads from local store — correct, but depends on store being synced first. (5) View materialization performance: O(N) over all messages in store — no index. Acceptable for current scale.

Implemented --fields flag on cf read. parseFieldSet validates field names, printMessagesWithFields does projection for human-readable output, encodeMessagesJSONWithFields for JSON output. nil fields = all fields (backward compat). 9 tests added covering valid/invalid fields, human output projection, JSON projection, and command-level error. All tests pass. Commit 8287e33.

Reviewed. Correctness: (1) --fields flag correctly filters output in both human-readable and JSON paths via parseFieldSet/printMessagesWithFields/encodeMessagesJSONWithFields. (2) Invalid fields return error early (before any I/O) — correct. (3) Default (empty string) returns nil fieldSet -> all fields printed — correct and backward compatible. (4) Works with --json via encodeMessagesJSONWithFields. (5) Human-readable path: projection correctly omits unrequested fields. All covered in read_fields_test.go. No critical issues in one-shot mode.

Reviewed. Edge cases: (1) Single field: works correctly for both human-readable and JSON paths. (2) All fields: works (nil fieldSet = all fields). (3) Empty string: returns nil = all fields — correct. (4) Duplicate fields: parseFieldSet uses a map, duplicates are deduplicated silently — acceptable. (5) Empty between commas: skipped silently — acceptable. (6) MAJOR: --fields ignored in --pull mode -> workspace-qem. (7) --fields with --follow: human-readable correctly threaded through fieldSet. JSON in follow mode: never triggered -> workspace-zci.

Reviewed. Code quality: (1) parseFieldSet is clean, well-named. (2) printMessagesWithFields: default path (nil fields) preserves original format exactly — good backward compatibility. (3) MINOR: projection path emits fmt.Println() unconditionally after each message even when no content printed -> workspace-3h9. (4) encodeMessagesJSONWithFields: clean map-based approach, tags nil guard is good. (5) validFieldNames map is defined at package level — correct. (6) Test coverage in read_fields_test.go: valid/invalid fields, empty, human-readable projection, JSON projection, --all fields, command-level error. Good coverage. Missing: --pull with --fields (not tested, and it's broken), follow+JSON+fields.

Reviewed. Integration: (1) Tag filters + --fields: works correctly in one-shot mode (sqlFilter applied first, then fieldSet for output). (2) Sender filter + --fields: same — correct. (3) --all + --fields: --all disables compaction filtering and cursor, --fields still applied — correct. (4) MAJOR: --fields ignored in --pull mode -> workspace-qem. (5) MAJOR: --follow + --json + --fields: JSON output path not triggered in follow loop -> workspace-zci. (6) Views + projection: view read has its own output path (outputViewProjected/outputViewJSON) separate from printMessagesWithFields — they don't share the --fields flag. This is intentional (views have their own projection in the definition) but could be confusing.
